spec_reconstruction.domain: Current U.S. federal banking board obligations for Category I global systemically important bank holding companies and their insured depository institution subsidiaries.
spec_reconstruction.unit_of_analysis: Each discrete current, in-force board-level obligation, with precise source, duty, actor allocation, committee or director-type distinction, and reform relevance.
structural_axes: entity tier: Category I GSIB BHC versus insured depository institution subsidiary
actor: full board versus board committee
committee type: audit, risk, compensation, nominating/governance, fiduciary, compliance, technology, or other designated committee
director type: inside, outside, independent, non-independent, risk-expert, interested director, and disqualified director distinctions
source tier: statute, regulation, enforceable guideline, supervisory guidance, examination manual, enforcement template, and interagency standard
issuing authority: Federal Reserve, OCC, FDIC, CFPB, FFIEC/interagency, FinCEN/OFAC where relevant

regulatory domain: safety and soundness, capital, stress testing, liquidity, recovery, resolution, operational resilience, cybersecurity, third-party risk, model risk, BSA/AML, consumer compliance, incentive compensation, audit, internal controls, fiduciary, affiliate, and insider-transaction governance
actor_classes_required: Category I GSIB BHC full board
insured depository institution subsidiary full board
source_universe: Federal Reserve regulations and supervisory guidance
OCC regulations, enforceable guidelines, bulletins, and manuals
FDIC regulations, guidance, examination material, and IDI resolution-planning rules
CFPB supervision and examination materials relevant to consumer compliance governance
Federal statutes underlying board-level banking obligations
coverage_areas: consumer compliance, fair lending, UDAAP, privacy, and CRA oversight
audit, internal control, financial reporting, fiduciary, insider, affiliate, and director-qualification obligations
spec_reconstruction.audience_use: Bank directors, legal counsel, and policymakers seeking a working reform document to identify duplicative, overlapping, and burdensome board-level requirements.
spec_reconstruction.form_depth_expectations: Granular, citation-specific 40-60 page inventory with current-law status, actor mapping, committee/director distinctions, and reform-useful sludge analysis.

material_omissions: The report expressly limits itself to 'representative, citation-specific obligations' rather than every obligation required by the original request.
The Category I GSIB list is incomplete: it names six BHCs and omits Bank of New York Mellon and State Street, both listed by the Federal Reserve as U.S. GSIBs as of February 2026.
It does not provide a row-by-row inventory of current obligations; it is a source map and narrative template.
BHC-versus-IDI subsidiary distinctions are introduced but not systematically applied to each obligation.
Audit, compensation, and nominating/governance committee obligations are thin and rely heavily on securities/listing standards rather than the requested federal banking source universe.
Inside/outside and independent/non-independent director distinctions are summarized generally, not tied to individual obligations.
Several required domains are shallow, especially recovery planning after OCC rescission, operational resilience, cybersecurity, consumer compliance, and incentive compensation.
The report uses only eight end citations and many bracketed source references without precise links or pinpoint citations.

results.citation: 12 CFR part 217 and 12 CFR part 252 / FSB G-SIB list for Category I firms
results.reason: The definition is broadly right, but the report's current firm list omits BNY Mellon and State Street; Federal Reserve lists eight U.S. GSIBs: https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/global-systemically-important-banks.htm

results.citation: Reg YY subparts E-Q as capital planning and stress testing board responsibility
results.reason: The board/designated committee capital-plan approval duty is in Regulation Y, 12 CFR 225.8, not Regulation YY subparts E-Q: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/regulations/section-2258-capital-planning-and-stress-capital-buffer-requirement.htm

results.citation: Regulation Q, 12 CFR part 217, GSIB surcharge
results.reason: Regulation Q supports GSIB surcharge and capital requirements, but the report overstates these as discrete board-level duties rather than firm obligations: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-12/chapter-II/subchapter-A/part-217/subpart-H

results.citation: TLAC/LTD rule, 12 CFR 252 subpart G
results.reason: Regulation YY subpart G applies TLAC/LTD requirements to U.S. GSIB BHCs; board-specific oversight is inferred, not directly stated: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/regulations/section-25263-external-total-loss-absorbing-capacity-requirement-and-buffer.htm

results.citation: FRB Commercial Bank Examination Manual, Section 5000.1
results.reason: The Fed manual supports broad director responsibilities for capital, policies, management supervision, condition of the bank, and compliance: https://www.federalreserve.gov/Boarddocs/SupManual/cbem/cbem_p5.pdf

results.citation: OCC Heightened Standards, 12 CFR part 30 Appendix D
results.reason: Appendix D supports risk-governance framework and board oversight duties, including active oversight and independent judgment: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-12/chapter-I/part-30/appendix-Appendix%20D%20to%20Part%2030

results.citation: OCC 12 CFR part 30 Appendix A/B; FDIC 12 CFR part 364 Appendix A/B
results.reason: Interagency safety-and-soundness and information-security standards support board/audit-committee oversight of controls, internal audit, and written information-security programs: https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/interagencyguidelines.htm

results.citation: FDIC Part 360/381 resolution and orderly liquidation standards for large IDIs
results.reason: 12 CFR 360.10 supports CIDI resolution submissions; Part 381 is not the IDI resolution-planning rule and 'orderly liquidation' framing is imprecise: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-12/chapter-III/subchapter-B/part-360/section-360.10

results.citation: CFPB Supervision and Examination Manual - CMS module
results.reason: CFPB CMR procedures support board and management oversight of CMS, though as examination guidance rather than binding law: https://www.consumerfinance.gov/compliance/supervision-examinations/compliance-management-review-examination-procedures/

results.citation: SR 11-7 Model Risk Management Guidance
results.reason: SR 11-7 historically supported the claim, but SR 26-2 superseded and replaced SR 11-7 on April 17, 2026: https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/srletters/SR2602.htm

results.citation: Interagency third-party risk management guidance
results.reason: The 2023 interagency guidance supports board/designated committee oversight and possible approval of higher-risk or critical-activity third-party plans: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/guidance/interagency-guidance-on-third-party-relationships.htm

results.citation: Interagency Guidance on Sound Incentive Compensation Policies (2010)
results.reason: The 2010 guidance supports board oversight of incentive compensation and risk alignment, but it is guidance, not binding regulation: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/guidance/guidance-on-sound-incentive-compensation-policies.htm

results.citation: GLBA Interagency Guidelines Establishing Information Security Standards
results.reason: The guidelines support the claim that the board or an appropriate committee must approve the written information-security program: https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/interagencyguidelines.htm

results.citation: BSA regulations and Interagency BSA/AML Examination Manual
results.reason: Federal Reserve BSA rule confirms written BSA program approval by the board and notation in minutes; parallel OCC/FDIC rules exist: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/regulations/section-20863-procedures-for-monitoring-bank-secrecy-act-compliance.htm

results.citation: Board must designate a qualified BSA Officer
results.reason: BSA rules require the compliance program to include designation of responsible individual(s), but do not clearly make designation itself a direct board act.

results.citation: 12 CFR 252.34 liquidity risk tolerance
results.reason: Section 252.34 expressly requires board approval of liquidity risk tolerance and liquidity risk-management strategies, policies, and procedures: https://www.law.cornell.edu/cfr/text/12/252.34

results.citation: 12 CFR 252.35 internal liquidity stress testing
results.reason: Section 252.35 supports liquidity stress-testing controls and oversight, but report should connect board duties mainly through 252.34 and risk committee responsibilities: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-12/chapter-II/subchapter-A/part-252/subpart-D/section-252.35

results.citation: Dodd-Frank section 165(d) / FRB-FDIC resolution plan rules
results.reason: Regulation QQ requires covered-company resolution plan approval by the board before submission and minutes notation: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/regulations/section-2434-resolution-plan-required.htm

results.citation: FDIC encouraged recovery planning for large IDIs; boards should review and approve recovery plans
results.reason: The current FDIC CIDI rule is resolution planning, not recovery planning; OCC recovery planning guidelines were rescinded effective 2026: https://occ.gov/news-issuances/news-releases/2026/nr-occ-2026-21.html

results.citation: Risk committees are composed mostly of independent directors under Reg YY/OCC standards
results.reason: Reg YY requires an independent chair and risk-experience member; the sampled sources do not support a general 'mostly independent' risk-committee requirement: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/regulations/section-25233-risk-management-and-risk-committee-requirements.htm

actor_mapping.note: The report frequently assigns duties to full board, risk committee, audit committee, compensation committee, and technology/compliance committees, but it does not create a complete obligation-by-actor ledger and only generally addresses director-type distinctions.

binding_vs_guidance.note: The report explicitly distinguishes binding, soft law, and persuasive authority, but still blends binding rules, guidance, international standards, securities/listing standards, and inferred oversight duties within the obligation descriptions.

reform_usefulness.note: The report includes a useful structure for building an inventory, but it has limited direct sludge analysis and mostly points to future work rather than producing reform-actionable obligation rows.

critical_flags.evidence: The report says "Reg YY subparts E-Q" cover capital planning and board review of capital plans.
The report cites "FDIC Part 360/381 resolution and orderly liquidation standards" for large IDIs.
The report lists "FRB SR 11-7 - Model Risk Management Guidance" as a key current source.
The report says "FDIC has encouraged recovery planning; boards should review and approve recovery plans".
The report says it gives "representative, citation-specific obligations" rather than every obligation.

D1_coverage.rationale: The report covers many required domains and starts with the right Category I framing, but it is not comprehensive, omits two current U.S. GSIBs, lacks an obligation-level ledger, and leaves several actor classes thin. F6 caps the effective score.

D2_integrity.evidence: The report says "Reg YY subparts E-Q" cover capital planning.
D2_integrity.rationale: Many sampled sources are real and partly supportive, but there are central misattributions, broad non-pinpoint references, and reliance on only eight end citations. F2 caps the effective score.

D3_accuracy.evidence: The report lists "FRB SR 11-7 - Model Risk Management Guidance".
D3_accuracy.rationale: The report is often directionally plausible, but it includes stale model-risk guidance, an unsupported recovery-planning board duty, incomplete Category I firm identification, and several overstated board-specific duties. Central F3/F4 caps D3 at zero.

D4_sludge_analysis.evidence: The report calls for "areas of overlapping or conflicting expectations".
D4_sludge_analysis.rationale: It provides a useful template for identifying overlap and committee allocation, but the actual sludge analysis is secondary to source mapping and does not fully specify reform levers by obligation.

D5_structure.evidence: The report offers a "Master source map".
D5_structure.rationale: The organization is clear and practical as a source map, but it is not a granular 40-60 page citation-level inventory and lacks row-level fields for each obligation.

D6_calibration.evidence: The report says the full task is "beyond what can be fully executed".
D6_calibration.rationale: The report is transparent about limitations and distinguishes authority tiers, which improves calibration. It still overstates some inferred duties as obligations.

strengths: D1/D5: The report correctly frames the target as Category I GSIB BHCs and IDI subsidiaries and covers many required domains at a source-map level.
D6: It is unusually transparent that a full 40-60 page inventory is beyond the response and distinguishes binding, soft-law, and persuasive sources.
D5: The proposed inventory grid is useful, with fields for domain, obligation, source, actor, director type, and notes.
D4: It identifies overlap points around capital/liquidity/resolution, operational resilience, model risk, third-party risk, BSA/AML, consumer compliance, and committee allocation.
weaknesses: D1: The report is not the requested inventory; it is a representative map and template, and it omits two current U.S. GSIBs from the Category I firm list.
D2/D3: Several central citations are inaccurate or stale, including Reg YY for capital-plan approval, FDIC Part 360/381 framing, SR 11-7 as current model-risk guidance, and FDIC recovery planning.
D5: It lacks a row-by-row ledger of discrete obligations with exact citations, current status, source type, actor, cadence, and documentation requirement.

D3: Several board duties are inferred from firm obligations or supervisory expectations without enough qualification, especially TLAC, liquidity stress testing, recovery planning, and BSA officer designation.

D1: Committee and director-type distinctions are summarized generally, with thin treatment of audit, compensation, and nominating/governance obligations under the requested banking authority universe.
prioritized_remediation: Correct the current U.S. GSIB list and separate top-tier BHC obligations from each IDI subsidiary obligation.

Replace broad or wrong citations with primary pinpoint citations, especially 12 CFR 225.8 for capital plans, 12 CFR 243.4/381 for BHC resolution plans, 12 CFR 360.10 for IDI resolution plans, 12 CFR 252.33-.35 for risk/liquidity, and SR 26-2 for model risk.
Convert the narrative into one obligation per row with columns for source type, binding status, actor, committee, director type, frequency, documentation, and reform lever.
Remove or clearly label inferred board oversight duties where the source imposes a firm or management obligation rather than an explicit board obligation.

Add full treatment of missing or thin domains: recovery/current resilience planning after OCC rescission, operational resilience, cybersecurity, audit, compensation committee, nominating/governance committee, and independent/director-type distinctions.
narrative.reliance_recommendation: Use this only as a preliminary source-map and drafting outline; do not rely on it as a current legal inventory without a full primary-source rebuild.
